content.text: Robust Error Handling & Recovery in Python Data Pipelines
Data pipelines are long-running, multi-stage systems that connect unreliable sources to downstream consumers — and failure is not a question of

. A pipeline without systematic error handling will silently corrupt data, lose records, or crash unrecoverably at 3am. The goal of error handling in data pipelines is not just to prevent crashes, but to make failures
Error handling in data pipelines refers to the complete discipline of
detecting, classifying, logging, recovering from, and alerting on failures
that occur during data extraction, transformation, loading, and any intermediate processing step. It encompasses:
— intercepting Python exceptions at the right granularity
— isolating bad records so the pipeline continues
— stopping cascade failures when a dependency is consistently down
— ensuring re-runs do not produce duplicate or inconsistent data
— structured logging, metrics, and tracing so failures can be diagnosed
— proactively notifying operators when thresholds are breached
A data pipeline that fails silently is more dangerous than one that crashes loudly. Downstream dashboards, ML models, and business reports depend on the
of the data they consume. Without error handling:
occurs when records are skipped without logging
bring down entire pipeline runs due to a single bad record
produce duplicate records in databases or data warehouses
go undetected until data quality audits catch them weeks later
means no one knows the pipeline is broken until users complain

[Extract] → [Validate] → [Transform] → [Enrich] → [Load] → [Audit]
    ↓             ↓            ↓            ↓          ↓         ↓
Network      Schema       Business      API        DB Write   Count
Timeouts     Violations   Logic Bugs    Rate Limit  Constraint  Mismatch
Auth Errors  Nulls        Type Errors   Timeout    Deadlock    Drift
(network timeouts, rate limits, temporary service unavailability) — should be retried with backoff.
(schema violations, malformed records, business logic rejections) — should be routed to a dead-letter queue and logged for human review.
(a dependency is completely down, a schema changed upstream) — should trigger circuit breaking and alerting.
Core Architecture: The Error Handling Stack

┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Pipeline Orchestrator          │  ← Airflow / Prefect / custom
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           Stage-Level Try/Except           │  ← Catch per stage, not globally
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         Record-Level Error Routing         │  ← Per-record try/except
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Retry Layer (with backoff)        │  ← tenacity / custom
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         Circuit Breaker Layer              │  ← Stop calling broken deps
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│       Dead-Letter Queue / Error Store      │  ← Isolate bad records
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│       Structured Logging & Metrics         │  ← JSON logs, Prometheus
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│       Alerting & Notification              │  ← PagerDuty, Slack, email
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The most important design decision is classifying exceptions correctly before writing any retry or routing logic.
instead of catching every specific exception type individually.
Retrying immediately on failure is almost always wrong — it can make rate limit problems worse and overload a recovering service. The correct pattern is

Without jitter, all failing clients retry at exactly the same time after a shared outage — the "thundering herd" — overwhelming the recovering service immediately. Jitter spreads the retry load over a time window.
Record-Level Error Routing and Dead-Letter Queues
Processing records one-by-one and routing bad records to a dead-letter store (rather than crashing the whole pipeline) is one of the highest-value patterns in data engineering.

import json
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any, Iterator
from pathlib import Path
from exceptions import PermanentError, TransientError

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class DeadLetterRecord:
    """A record that failed processing, preserved for inspection and replay."""
    original_record: dict
    error_type: str
    error_message: str
    stage: str
    timestamp: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())
    attempt_count: int = 1
    traceback: str = ""


class DeadLetterQueue:
    """
    Writes failed records to a JSONL file for later inspection and replay.
    In production this would write to S3, Kafka, or a database table.
    """

    def __init__(self, path: Path):
        self.path = path
        self.path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        self._count = 0

    def send(self, record: DeadLetterRecord) -> None:
        with self.path.open("a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps(asdict(record)) + "\n")
        self._count += 1
        logger.warning(
            "DLQ | stage=%s error_type=%s record_id=%s",
            record.stage,
            record.error_type,
            record.original_record.get("id", "unknown"),
        )

    @property
    def count(self) -> int:
        return self._count
Now use the DLQ inside a processing loop:

import traceback

def transform_record(record: dict) -> dict:
    """Business logic transformation — raises PermanentError on bad data."""
    if "user_id" not in record:
        raise SchemaValidationError("Missing required field 'user_id'", record=record, field="user_id")
    if not isinstance(record.get("amount"), (int, float)):
        raise SchemaValidationError("Field 'amount' must be numeric", record=record, field="amount")
    if record["amount"] < 0:
        raise BusinessRuleViolation(f"Negative amount not allowed: {record['amount']}")

    return {
        "user_id": str(record["user_id"]),
        "amount_cents": int(record["amount"] * 100),
        "processed_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }


def process_batch(
    records: list[dict],
    dlq: DeadLetterQueue,
    stage: str = "transform",
) -> tuple[list[dict], int]:
    """
    Process a batch of records.
    Returns (successful_records, failed_count).
    Permanent failures go to DLQ; transient failures are re-raised to trigger stage retry.
    """
    successful = []
    failed = 0

    for record in records:
        try:
            result = transform_record(record)
            successful.append(result)

        except PermanentError as e:
            # Route to DLQ — do not crash the pipeline
            dlq.send(DeadLetterRecord(
                original_record=record,
                error_type=type(e).__name__,
                error_message=str(e),
                stage=stage,
                traceback=traceback.format_exc(),
            ))
            failed += 1

        except TransientError:
            # Re-raise — the stage-level retry logic should handle this
            raise

        except Exception as e:
            # Unexpected error — treat as permanent, log with full context
            logger.error(
                "Unexpected error processing record id=%s: %s",
                record.get("id"), e, exc_info=True,
            )
            dlq.send(DeadLetterRecord(
                original_record=record,
                error_type=type(e).__name__,
                error_message=str(e),
                stage=stage,
                traceback=traceback.format_exc(),
            ))
            failed += 1

    logger.info(
        "Batch processed | stage=%s success=%d failed=%d",
        stage, len(successful), failed,
    )
    return successful, failed
A circuit breaker prevents a pipeline from hammering a downstream dependency that is clearly broken. It transitions through three states:

CLOSED (normal) ──[failures > threshold]──► OPEN (blocking)
    ▲                                              │
    └──[half-open probe succeeds]──── HALF-OPEN ◄─┘
                                    (test one request)

import time
from enum import Enum, auto
from threading import Lock


class CircuitState(Enum):
    CLOSED = auto()    # Normal operation
    OPEN = auto()      # Blocking all calls
    HALF_OPEN = auto() # Testing if recovery occurred


class CircuitBreaker:
    """
    Wraps a callable and opens the circuit after too many consecutive failures.
    Automatically attempts recovery after a cooldown period.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        failure_threshold: int = 5,
        recovery_timeout: float = 60.0,
        success_threshold: int = 2,
    ):
        self.failure_threshold = failure_threshold
        self.recovery_timeout = recovery_timeout
        self.success_threshold = success_threshold

        self._state = CircuitState.CLOSED
        self._failure_count = 0
        self._success_count = 0
        self._opened_at: float | None = None
        self._lock = Lock()

    @property
    def state(self) -> CircuitState:
        with self._lock:
            if self._state == CircuitState.OPEN:
                if time.monotonic() - self._opened_at >= self.recovery_timeout:
                    self._state = CircuitState.HALF_OPEN
                    self._success_count = 0
                    logger.info("Circuit breaker entering HALF_OPEN state")
            return self._state

    def call(self, func, *args, **kwargs):
        state = self.state
        if state == CircuitState.OPEN:
            raise SystemicError("Circuit breaker is OPEN — dependency unavailable")

        try:
            result = func(*args, **kwargs)
            self._on_success()
            return result
        except (TransientError, SystemicError) as e:
            self._on_failure()
            raise

    def _on_success(self):
        with self._lock:
            if self._state == CircuitState.HALF_OPEN:
                self._success_count += 1
                if self._success_count >= self.success_threshold:
                    self._state = CircuitState.CLOSED
                    self._failure_count = 0
                    logger.info("Circuit breaker CLOSED — dependency recovered")
            else:
                self._failure_count = 0

    def _on_failure(self):
        with self._lock:
            self._failure_count += 1
            if (self._state == CircuitState.HALF_OPEN or
                    self._failure_count >= self.failure_threshold):
                self._state = CircuitState.OPEN
                self._opened_at = time.monotonic()
                logger.error(
                    "Circuit breaker OPENED after %d failures",
                    self._failure_count,
                )


# Usage
db_circuit = CircuitBreaker(failure_threshold=5, recovery_timeout=30.0)

def write_to_database(records: list[dict]) -> None:
    db_circuit.call(_do_db_write, records)

def _do_db_write(records: list[dict]) -> None:
    # actual DB write logic here
    pass
Plain text logs are nearly useless at scale. Structured JSON logs can be queried, aggregated, and alerted on by tools like Elasticsearch, Datadog, Splunk, or CloudWatch.

configure_pipeline_logging()
logger = logging.getLogger("pipeline.transform")

logger.info(
    "Stage complete",
    extra={
        "pipeline_run_id": "run-2024-abc123",
        "pipeline_stage": "transform",
        "pipeline_records_processed": 10000,
        "pipeline_records_failed": 3,
        "pipeline_duration_seconds": 4.7,
    }
)

from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge, start_http_server
import time

# Define metrics at module level (singleton)
RECORDS_PROCESSED = Counter(
    "pipeline_records_processed_total",
    "Total number of records processed",
    ["stage", "status"],   # labels: stage=transform, status=success|failed
)

STAGE_DURATION = Histogram(
    "pipeline_stage_duration_seconds",
    "Time spent in each pipeline stage",
    ["stage"],
    buckets=[0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0, 60.0, 120.0],
)

DLQ_SIZE = Gauge(
    "pipeline_dlq_size",
    "Current number of records in the dead-letter queue",
    ["stage"],
)

CIRCUIT_BREAKER_STATE = Gauge(
    "pipeline_circuit_breaker_open",
    "1 if circuit breaker is open, 0 otherwise",
    ["dependency"],
)


class InstrumentedStage:
    """Context manager that records duration and outcome metrics for a stage."""

    def __init__(self, stage_name: str):
        self.stage_name = stage_name
        self._start = None

    def __enter__(self):
        self._start = time.monotonic()
        return self

    def record_success(self, count: int = 1):
        RECORDS_PROCESSED.labels(stage=self.stage_name, status="success").inc(count)

    def record_failure(self, count: int = 1):
        RECORDS_PROCESSED.labels(stage=self.stage_name, status="failed").inc(count)

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        duration = time.monotonic() - self._start
        STAGE_DURATION.labels(stage=self.stage_name).observe(duration)
        return False  # Do not suppress exceptions


# Usage
with InstrumentedStage("transform") as stage:
    successful, failed = process_batch(records, dlq)
    stage.record_success(len(successful))
    stage.record_failure(failed)
    DLQ_SIZE.labels(stage="transform").set(dlq.count)
if running it multiple times produces the same result as running it once. This is critical because any retry mechanism, orchestrator restart, or manual re-run will re-execute the pipeline.

import psycopg2

def upsert_records(conn, records: list[dict], run_id: str) -> int:
    """
    Load records using UPSERT — safe to call multiple times with the same data.
    Uses run_id to prevent processing the same pipeline run twice.
    """
    upserted = 0
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        for record in records:
            cur.execute("""
                INSERT INTO processed_events (
                    event_id, user_id, amount_cents, processed_at, pipeline_run_id
                )
                VALUES (%(event_id)s, %(user_id)s, %(amount_cents)s, %(processed_at)s, %(run_id)s)
                ON CONFLICT (event_id) DO UPDATE SET
                    amount_cents = EXCLUDED.amount_cents,
                    processed_at = EXCLUDED.processed_at,
                    pipeline_run_id = EXCLUDED.pipeline_run_id
                WHERE processed_events.pipeline_run_id != EXCLUDED.pipeline_run_id
            """, {**record, "run_id": run_id})
            upserted += cur.rowcount
    conn.commit()
    return upserted
For long-running pipelines reading from a source with offsets (Kafka, S3 files, database cursors), only advance the checkpoint
Complete Pipeline: Putting It All Together

import logging
import sys
import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Iterator

configure_pipeline_logging()
logger = logging.getLogger("pipeline.main")


@dataclass
class PipelineResult:
    run_id: str
    total_records: int
    successful: int
    failed: int
    dlq_count: int
    duration_seconds: float
    status: str  # "success" | "partial" | "failed"


def run_pipeline(source_records: list[dict]) -> PipelineResult:
    run_id = f"run-{datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime('%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')}-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
    dlq = DeadLetterQueue(Path(f"dlq/{run_id}.jsonl"))
    db_circuit = CircuitBreaker(failure_threshold=5, recovery_timeout=30.0)
    checkpoint = CheckpointManager(Path("checkpoints/pipeline.json"))
    start_time = time.monotonic()

    logger.info("Pipeline started", extra={"pipeline_run_id": run_id})

    total_successful = 0
    total_failed = 0
    batch_size = 500

    try:
        for batch_start in range(0, len(source_records), batch_size):
            batch = source_records[batch_start: batch_start + batch_size]

            with InstrumentedStage("transform") as stage:
                successful_records, failed_count = process_batch(batch, dlq, stage="transform")
                stage.record_success(len(successful_records))
                stage.record_failure(failed_count)

            total_failed += failed_count

            # Load with circuit breaker protection
            try:
                with InstrumentedStage("load") as stage:
                    upserted = db_circuit.call(
                        upsert_records,
                        db_conn,
                        successful_records,
                        run_id,
                    )
                    total_successful += upserted
                    stage.record_success(upserted)

            except SystemicError as e:
                # Circuit is open — abort the pipeline run, preserve checkpoint
                logger.error(
                    "Aborting pipeline: circuit breaker open. Will resume from checkpoint.",
                    extra={"pipeline_run_id": run_id},
                    exc_info=True,
                )
                raise

            # Save checkpoint after each successful batch
            checkpoint.save({
                "run_id": run_id,
                "last_batch_start": batch_start + batch_size,
                "total_successful": total_successful,
            })

    except SystemicError:
        status = "failed"
    except Exception as e:
        logger.critical("Unhandled pipeline error", exc_info=True,
                        extra={"pipeline_run_id": run_id})
        status = "failed"
    else:
        failure_rate = total_failed / max(len(source_records), 1)
        status = "success" if failure_rate < 0.01 else "partial"

    duration = time.monotonic() - start_time
    result = PipelineResult(
        run_id=run_id,
        total_records=len(source_records),
        successful=total_successful,
        failed=total_failed,
        dlq_count=dlq.count,
        duration_seconds=duration,
        status=status,
    )
    logger.info(
        "Pipeline complete",
        extra={
            "pipeline_run_id": run_id,
            "pipeline_status": status,
            "pipeline_duration_seconds": duration,
            "pipeline_success_count": total_successful,
            "pipeline_failed_count": total_failed,
        }
    )
    return result
Dead-letter records should be replayable once the root cause is fixed:

def replay_dlq(dlq_path: Path, pipeline_func) -> PipelineResult:
    """Re-submit all DLQ records through the pipeline after fixing the root cause."""
    records = []
    with dlq_path.open() as f:
        for line in f:
            entry = json.loads(line)
            records.append(entry["original_record"])

    logger.info("Replaying %d DLQ records from %s", len(records), dlq_path)
    result = pipeline_func(records)

    if result.status == "success":
        # Archive the DLQ file instead of deleting
        dlq_path.rename(dlq_path.with_suffix(".replayed.jsonl"))

    return result
too broadly at the top level.
turns all errors into the same undifferentiated blob, losing the ability to retry transients, route permanents, or detect systemics differently. Always classify first.
Schema violations, business rule failures, and malformed records will fail
time. Retrying them wastes time, inflates error metrics, and delays detecting the actual root cause.
is the single most dangerous pattern in any pipeline. It creates silent data loss with zero visibility. Always log with

If a write operation succeeds but the network drops before the acknowledgment returns, the caller sees a failure and retries — inserting the record a second time. Always use upsert semantics or idempotency keys.
Saving checkpoints before confirming the write succeeded.
If the checkpoint advances before the write completes and the process crashes, those records are permanently lost. Commit writes first, then advance the checkpoint.
Advancing the checkpoint in the wrong order.
The sequence must always be: read → process → write → checkpoint. Any other order loses at-least-once delivery guarantees.
Uncapped exponential backoff means a retry after 10 failed attempts could wait
Build a custom exception hierarchy as the very first step of any pipeline project. Accurate classification is what makes all downstream handling correct.
level with full tracebacks and trigger immediate alerts. Unknown errors are more dangerous than known ones.
Design for at-least-once delivery; handle duplicates at the destination.
It is simpler and more reliable to guarantee every record is processed
once (via checkpoints and retries) and deduplicate at the load target than to guarantee exactly-once delivery in the pipeline itself.
Set DLQ size thresholds as alerts.
If more than 1–5% of records are hitting the DLQ in a single run, that indicates a systematic problem (schema change upstream, business logic regression) that requires human intervention. Alert on
in every log entry and every loaded record.
This makes it possible to trace exactly which pipeline run produced which records, replay a specific run's DLQ, and correlate logs with metrics.

into your pipeline and assert that retries fire, DLQ entries are written, and the circuit breaker transitions states correctly. Failure handling that is not tested will fail incorrectly in production.
Use JSONL (newline-delimited JSON) for DLQ files.
It is appendable, streamable, and trivially parseable. It does not require loading the entire file into memory for replay, making it suitable for large DLQ files.
Retry logic with decorators, backoff strategies, and pluggable conditions
In-process metrics exposition for Prometheus scraping
Structured logging with context binding and processors
Distributed tracing across pipeline stages and services
DAG-based orchestration with built-in retry, alerting, and SLA tracking
Python-native orchestration with automatic state tracking and DLQ concepts
Data-aware orchestration with asset lineage and built-in observability
Automatic exception capture with context and release tracking
Schema validation at record ingestion boundaries
Data quality assertions integrated into pipeline stages
Streaming pipelines with native DLQ support (dead-letter topics)
Think of a data pipeline as an
— but one that handles defective parts:
move down the line and are assembled normally.
(transient failures) cause the line to pause briefly and retry, then continue.
(permanent failures) are pulled off the line and placed in a
(DLQ) — they don't stop the factory, but they are tracked, inspected, and potentially reworked later.
that stops the whole line from trying to use that machine until maintenance confirms it is fixed.
(metrics and structured logs) tell supervisors in real time how many parts are being produced, how many are defective, and whether any machine is about to break.
The line keeps running even when individual defects occur, the defects are not lost, and operators always know exactly what is happening.
Classify exceptions as transient, permanent, or systemic
before writing any handling logic — correct classification drives all downstream decisions about retries, routing, and aborting.
, always with exponential backoff and jitter, and always with a hard limit on attempts.
Route permanent failures to a dead-letter queue
rather than crashing or skipping silently — the DLQ preserves bad records for inspection and replay.
to prevent a failing dependency from consuming all pipeline resources and to allow automatic recovery detection.
: every write operation must be safe to repeat, and checkpoints must only advance
Structured JSON logging with a consistent
is the foundation of pipeline observability — it turns error investigation from guesswork into a query.
as rigorously as happy paths — untested error handling will silently break exactly when it is needed most.
